section.title: Renewed Praise of the Bride
section.sectionRationale: After the night of seeking, the bridegroom returns to praise. He calls his bride as beautiful as Tirzah, as lovely as Jerusalem, and overwhelming as an army with banners. Among all the queens and concubines she is his one dove, his perfect one, unique. The friends ask, who is this who shines like the dawn, and the movement ends with the call, come back, O Shulammite, that we may gaze upon you.
section.thesis: The bridegroom renews his praise of the bride as uniquely beautiful and wholly his own, one dove among all the rest, and both friends and beloved marvel at her radiance as she shines like the dawn.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the bridegroom overcome by his darling's beauty, more overwhelming than an army; his one dove, unique among all the queens, whom even they call blessed; and the wondering question, who is this who shines like the dawn, with the call for the Shulammite to return.

units.label: As beautiful as Tirzah, overwhelming as an army

verses.text: You are as beautiful, my {{raya|darling}}, as Tirzah, as lovely as Jerusalem, as majestic as troops with banners.
Turn your eyes away from me, for they have overcome me. Your hair is like a flock of goats streaming down from Gilead.
Your teeth are like a flock of sheep coming up from the washing; each has its twin, and not one of them is lost.
Your brow behind your veil is like a slice of pomegranate.

units.label: My dove, my perfect one, is unique

verses.text: There are sixty queens and eighty concubines, and maidens without number,
but my dove, my {{tammah|perfect one}}, is unique, the favorite of the mother who bore her. The maidens see her and call her blessed; the queens and concubines sing her praises.

units.label: Who is this who shines like the dawn

verses.text: Who is this who shines like the dawn, as fair as the moon, as bright as the sun, as majestic as the stars in procession?
I went down to the walnut grove to see the blossoms of the valley, to see if the vines were budding or the pomegranates were in bloom.
Before I realized it, my desire had set me among the royal chariots of my people.
Come back, come back, O {{shulammite|Shulammite}}! Come back, come back, that we may gaze upon you. Why do you look at the Shulammite, as on the dance of Mahanaim?

raya.gloss: My darling, literally my companion or dear friend, the bridegroom's recurring name for the bride. Here it opens his renewed praise, you are as beautiful, my darling, as Tirzah.

tammah.gloss: My perfect one, my flawless one. The bridegroom calls the bride his dove, his perfect one, unique among all the queens and maidens; the word carries wholeness and integrity, one wholly his and without flaw in his eyes.

shulammite.gloss: The Shulammite, the only proper name given the bride in the Song. It may point to her home town or echo the word for peace, shalom; the friends call her back so they may gaze upon her radiance.

basic.context: After the anguish of the night search, the bridegroom returns with fresh praise. He tells his bride she is as beautiful as the loveliest cities, Tirzah and Jerusalem, and as overwhelming as an army with banners; her gaze undoes him. Among all the queens and concubines of a royal court she stands out as his one dove, his perfect one, unique, and even they call her blessed. Then a wondering voice asks, who is this who shines like the dawn, and the movement closes with the cry, come back, O Shulammite, that we may gaze upon you.
basic.hermeneutics: On the plain level this is a husband's renewed delight in his wife after conflict and separation, love that returns to praise rather than blame. The imagery is poetic: cities, armies, sun and moon, all to say she is beautiful, singular, and dazzling. Within the whole Bible, the church has heard here the beauty the Lord sees in His people, one bride singled out from among the many, radiant not by her own glory but by His.
basic.originalLanguages: The man calls the bride raya, my darling, his companion, and then tammah, my perfect one, his dove, unique among all the rest. She is given the name Shulammite, the only name she bears in the Song, perhaps tied to peace, shalom. The friends ask who is this who shines like the dawn, a question of wonder at her borrowed radiance.
basic.typology: The one dove singled out from among the many queens pictures the church chosen and set apart, the one the Lord calls unique and blessed. The bride who shines like the dawn foreshadows the people who reflect the glory of their Lord, fair as the moon and bright as the sun.
basic.christ: The bridegroom's renewed praise after failure pictures the love of Christ that returns to His people not with reproach but with delight, calling them beautiful and unique still. The bride who shines like the dawn is the church made radiant with a borrowed light, reflecting the glory of the Lord who never turns His love away.
basic.god: God does not love His people grudgingly or for a season; He delights in them, calls them unique among all the world, and returns to praise even after they have failed Him. His steadfast love makes the beloved radiant, so that she shines with a light that is really His.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 5:27, the church presented in splendor, without spot. Zephaniah 3:17, the LORD will rejoice over you with singing. 2 Corinthians 3:18, beholding His glory, we are transformed into the same image. Matthew 13:45 to 46, the one pearl of great price.
basic.oneStory: After the night of failure and searching, love returns to praise: the bridegroom names his bride unique and radiant, one dove among all the rest. It pictures the unwavering delight of Christ in His church, whom He makes to shine with His own glory.

context.literary: A renewed wasf, the bridegroom praising the bride again, echoing much of chapter 4 but now after the estrangement of the night. It moves from praise, to the declaration of her uniqueness, to the wondering question who is this, and ends on the disputed, dance like scene of the Shulammite's return, a bridge into the fuller praise of chapter 7.
context.historical: Tirzah and Jerusalem were the two most beautiful royal cities of Israel; an army with banners evokes ordered, dazzling might. The sixty queens and eighty concubines picture a great royal harem, against which the one dove stands out as singular. The name Shulammite and the dance of Mahanaim remain debated in detail.
context.canonical: The theme of one bride singled out from among the many, and made radiant by her lord, runs through Scripture, from Israel chosen from the nations, to the church presented in splendor, to the new Jerusalem shining with the glory of God, her lamp the Lamb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To renew the bridegroom's praise of the bride after separation, declaring her uniquely beautiful and wholly his own, one dove among all the rest, and marveling at her radiance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the bridegroom's delight rather than issuing commands, yet it carries an abiding vision: covenant love that returns to praise after failure and prizes the beloved as singular and set apart.

debates.question: What kind of book is the Song: a celebration of married love, an allegory of Christ and the church, or both?

views.label: A celebration of covenant married love
views.case: This is plainly a husband's renewed delight in his wife, prizing her as unique among all women; Scripture commends such singular, faithful affection within marriage, Proverbs 5:18 to 19 and Malachi 2:14 to 15.

views.label: An allegory of Christ and the church, or God and Israel
views.case: The church has long heard here the Lord's love for His one people, singled out from among the many and made radiant, in keeping with Israel chosen from the nations and the church presented in splendor, Ephesians 5:27.

views.label: Both, the literal love as a God-given sign
views.case: The wisest reading holds both: the poem truly celebrates a husband's renewed praise of his singular wife, and because marriage images Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32, his one dove made radiant rightly pictures the church set apart and shining with her Lord's glory.

debates.bearing: Holding both keeps the passage true: it honors a husband's singular delight in his wife and follows it to Christ, whose love returns to His people and makes them shine with a borrowed light.

study.originalLanguages: The bridegroom opens with raya, my darling, his companion, and then names her tammah, my perfect one, his dove, unique among sixty queens and eighty concubines and maidens without number. The one name she bears in the whole Song is Shulammite, perhaps rooted in shalom, peace. The friends' cry, who is this who shines like the dawn, fair as the moon, bright as the sun, wonders at a radiance she reflects rather than owns, the light of the one who loves her.

typology.type: The one dove, unique among all the queens and maidens
typology.fulfillment: The church chosen and set apart from among the many, the one pearl of great price, Matthew 13:45 to 46.

typology.type: The bride who shines like the dawn, fair as the moon, bright as the sun
typology.fulfillment: The church made radiant with the reflected glory of her Lord, transformed as she beholds Him, 2 Corinthians 3:18.

typology.type: Praise renewed after the night of failure
typology.fulfillment: The love of Christ that returns to His people not to reproach but to delight in them and restore them, Zephaniah 3:17.

study.christ: The bridegroom's return is grace: after the night when the bride was slow to open and then could not find him, he comes back not with rebuke but with praise, calling her beautiful, unique, his one dove. This is the love of Christ that does not turn away from His failing people but restores them and delights in them still. And when the friends ask, who is this who shines like the dawn, the answer is a bride radiant with borrowed light, the church reflecting the glory of the Lord she beholds, made bright not by her own goodness but by His.
study.god: God's delight in His people is not fickle or earned; He prizes them as unique among all the world and returns to praise even after they have failed Him. His steadfast love is what makes the beloved beautiful, and His glory is the light in which she shines. He rejoices over His people with singing, and He is making them radiant for the day He presents them to Himself.

crossReferences.note: Christ presents the church to Himself in splendor, holy and without blemish, the singular radiant bride.
The LORD your God rejoices over you with gladness and singing, love that returns to delight.
Beholding the glory of the Lord, we are transformed into the same image, the bride shining with borrowed light.
The one pearl of great price, singled out and treasured, like the one dove among all the rest.

study.oneStory: Love returns to praise: after the night of failure the bridegroom names his bride unique, beautiful, his one dove, and both friends and beloved marvel that she shines like the dawn. It pictures the unwavering delight of Christ in His church, whom He restores after failure and makes radiant with His own glory.

quiz.q: To what does the bridegroom compare the bride's beauty in 6:4?

options.text: To the lovely cities Tirzah and Jerusalem, and to an army with banners.
options.note: He likens her to the most beautiful cities and to dazzling, ordered might.

options.text: To a ruined and abandoned town.
options.note: He does compare her to a city, but to the loveliest cities, Tirzah and Jerusalem, not to a ruined and abandoned town.

options.text: To an ordinary field.
options.note: He does draw on the created world for his images, but here he reaches for the most striking, cities and an army with banners, not an ordinary field.

options.text: To a fading flower.
options.note: His praise is of overwhelming beauty, not decline.

quiz.q: How does the bridegroom set the bride apart from all the queens and maidens in 6:9?

options.text: She is his one dove, his perfect one, unique, and even they call her blessed.
options.note: She is singled out as unique among all the rest.

options.text: She is merely one of many equals.
options.note: She is truly set among the many queens and maidens, but he singles her out as his one perfect dove, unique, not one equal among them.

options.text: She is the least among them.
options.note: She is prized above all, his one perfect dove.

options.text: She is admired only for a moment.
options.note: She is indeed admired, but his delight in her is settled and lasting, and even the queens call her blessed, not admired only for a moment.

quiz.q: What wondering question do the friends ask in 6:10?

options.text: Who is this who shines like the dawn, fair as the moon and bright as the sun?
options.note: They marvel at her radiance, a light she reflects rather than owns.

options.text: Who is this who hides in the dark?
options.note: It echoes their wondering, who is this, but they marvel that she shines like the dawn, not that she hides in the dark.

options.text: Why is this woman so plain?
options.note: The wonder is at her dazzling beauty, not plainness.

options.text: Where has this stranger come from?
options.note: It rightly echoes their wondering, who is this, but their marvel is at her radiance, shining like the dawn, not merely at where she came from.

quiz.q: How does the bridegroom's renewed praise picture the love of Christ?

options.text: He returns after the night of failure to delight in his bride, not to reproach her.
options.note: Christ's love restores His failing people and delights in them still.

options.text: He waits until she has made herself perfect before loving her again.
options.note: He does return to her after her failure in the night, but not because she has first made herself perfect; he praises her freely, and her radiance is borrowed, reflected light.

options.text: He abandons her for her failure in the night.
options.note: The opposite: he returns with praise and restoration.

options.text: He loves her only while she is useful.
options.note: His love does bless and honor her, but it is settled love that calls her unique and blessed, not love that lasts only while she is useful.

quiz.q: What does the bride shining like the dawn picture in the whole Bible?

options.text: The church made radiant with the reflected glory of her Lord.
options.note: She shines with borrowed light, as believers are transformed beholding His glory.

options.text: That believers glow by their own goodness.
options.note: Believers do truly shine, but with light reflected from their Lord, not with a glow of their own goodness.

options.text: That God's people have no glory at all.
options.note: Her glory is indeed not her own, but she truly does shine, fair as the moon and bright as the sun, with His borrowed light, so it is not that she has no glory at all.

options.text: That beauty is worthless to God.
options.note: The passage celebrates the beauty God gives His beloved.

questions: The bridegroom returns after the night of failure with praise, not blame. How does it move you that Christ comes back to His failing people to delight in them rather than reproach them?
Among all the queens the bride is his one dove, unique. Have you let yourself believe that the Lord prizes you as singular and set apart, not lost in a crowd?
Who is this who shines like the dawn? The bride's radiance is borrowed from her beloved. Where might you be trying to shine by your own light instead of reflecting His?
The bridegroom says her eyes overwhelm him. How does knowing that the Lord delights in His people change the way you come to Him?
The queens and maidens end up singing her praises. How can you speak well of the beauty God has worked in someone else this week, rather than envying it?

translationNotes.note: Tirzah and Jerusalem were prized royal cities; troops with banners renders an image of ordered, dazzling might, sometimes translated an army with banners.
This is one of the most difficult verses in the Song; the sense of my desire had set me among the royal chariots is uncertain, and a footnote offers among the chariots of Amminadab.
Shulammite is the only proper name given the bride, perhaps tied to shalom, peace; the dance of Mahanaim, or dance of the two camps, remains debated.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Song of Solomon 6:4-13, chapter 6. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment text values in order; embedded speaker labels such as The Friends and The Bridegroom were treated as headings and excluded. Footnote markers were ignored. No em or en dashes occur in this section; none remain.
